1: τί οὖν τὸ περισσὸν τοῦ Ἰουδαίου (ti oun to perisson tou Ioudaiou) — "what then is the advantage of the Jew?" — Paul shifts to a diatribal interlocutor. Perisson means "excess, advantage, surplus" — the same root as perisseuo (to abound). The question acknowledges that the argument of Romans 2 might seem to eliminate Jewish advantage entirely; Paul will affirm real advantage before qualifying it.
2: ἐπιστεύθησαν τὰ λόγια τοῦ θεοῦ (episteuthēsan ta logia tou theou) — "they were entrusted with the oracles of God" — the aorist passive of pisteuō (to believe/entrust); the same word-family as faith but in the trust-deposit sense. Logia (oracles) is a term for divine utterances, particularly prophetic speech. The advantage is not ethnicity but covenant revelation: having been entrusted with God's own words.
3: ἡ ἀπιστία αὐτῶν τὴν πίστιν τοῦ θεοῦ καταργήσει (hē apistia autōn tēn pistin tou theou katargēsei) — "will their unbelief nullify God's faithfulness?" — apistia (unbelief/unfaithfulness) contrasted with pistis tou theou (faithfulness of God). The genitive tou theou here is clearly subjective: God's own faithfulness/trustworthiness. Katargeō — "to render inoperative, nullify" — a strong verb Paul uses for the abolition of the law and the nullification of Christ (Galatians 5:4).
4: γινέσθω δὲ ὁ θεὸς ἀληθής (ginesthō de ho theos alēthēs) — "let God be true" — the imperative ginesthō (become/let-be) does not mean God is not yet true but is the rhetorical assertion of the absolute: even if (hypothetically) all humans were liars, God's truth stands unaffected. ὅπως ἂν δικαιωθῇς ἐν τοῖς λόγοις σου — quoting LXX Psalms 51:4: "that you may be justified in your words" — the psalmist's confession that God is right when he judges applies to the present argument.
5: τί ἐροῦμεν; μὴ ἄδικος ὁ θεός (ti eroumen; mē adikos ho theos) — "what shall we say? Is God unjust?" — the interlocutor's reductio: if my injustice displays God's righteousness, how can God judge me? Paul identifies the reasoning immediately as distinctively human (κατὰ ἄνθρωπον λέγω — "I speak as a human being"). The objection is logically coherent but morally absurd.
6: ἐπεὶ πῶς κρινεῖ ὁ θεὸς τὸν κόσμον — "since how will God judge the world?" — the rhetorical appeal to universal divine judgment as self-evident: if God cannot judge fairly, the entire structure of biblical theodicy collapses. The world's judgment as God's prerogative is so foundational that the objection refutes itself.
7: ἐν τῷ ἐμῷ ψεύσματι (en tō emō pseudismati) — "through my lie" — Paul continues the diatribal exchange, now pushing the logic to its most absurd conclusion: if my falsehood increases God's truth-display, I should be more praised, not condemned. The ema (my own) is emphatic: this is the interlocutor's own reasoning about his own falsehood.
8: ποιήσωμεν τὰ κακὰ ἵνα ἔλθῃ τὰ ἀγαθά — "let us do evil that good may come" — this is not Paul's teaching but the slander leveled against Paul's gospel of grace; some were evidently claiming that his teaching logically implied moral libertinism. ὧν τὸ κρίμα ἔνδικόν ἐστιν — "whose condemnation is just" — endikos (in-right, just) pronounces the condemnation fitting by the law's own standard.
9:
οὐ πάντως (ou pantōs) — "not at all / not entirely." The phrase is debated: does Paul say Jews have "no advantage at all" (contradicting v2) or "no advantage in every respect"? Given v2's "much in every way," Paul likely means "no decisive advantage for the purpose of avoiding condemnation" — not a flat denial of all Jewish advantage.

προῃτιασάμεθα (proētiasametha) — "we have already charged" — the perfect tense indicates the charge is established from prior argument; Paul now summarizes. The verb aitiaō is forensic: to bring a formal charge.
10: The catena of quotations in vv10-18 is a classical Jewish rhetorical technique of stringing OT texts together to make a cumulative point (cf. 4QFlor at Qumran). The individual quotations are from Ps 14/53; Psalms 5:9; Psalms 140:3; Psalms 10:7; Isaiah 59:7-8; Psalms 36:1 — spanning Psalms and Prophets to demonstrate that the indictment is not Pauline invention but Scripture's own verdict on universal human unrighteousness.
11: οὐκ ἔστιν ὁ συνίων, οὐκ ἔστιν ὁ ἐκζητῶν τὸν θεόν — "there is none who understands, none who seeks God" — syniōn (understanding, from synesis) and ekzētōn (seeking out, intensive compound of zēteō). The double negation and the present participles make these absolute characterizations: understanding and God-seeking are entirely absent from human initiative in the natural state. Paul quotes Psalms 14:2 LXX verbatim.
12: πάντες ἐξέκλιναν (pantes exeklinan) — "all have turned aside" — the aorist active: a completed definitive turning. Exklinō (turn out from, deviate) is a navigational metaphor: everyone has gone off course. ἅμα (hama) — "together/at once" — universality is both numerical (all) and simultaneous (together), leaving no exception.
13: τάφος ἀνεῳγμένος (taphos aneōgmenos) — "an open grave" — perfect passive participle: permanently open. The throat as an open grave is the image of consuming speech that destroys what it consumes; Psalms 5:9 LXX provides this image. ἐδολιοῦσαν ταῖς γλώσσαις αὐτῶν (edoliousan tais glōssais autōn) — "with their tongues they were deceitful" — imperfect tense: ongoing habitual deceit. The speech-anatomy chain (throat, tongue, lips, mouth) moves from the largest organ to the smallest.
14: οὗ τὸ στόμα ἀρᾶς καὶ πικρίας γέμει (hou to stoma aras kai pikrias gemei) — "whose mouth is full of cursing and bitterness" — the present active gemei (is full to the brim) indicates a saturated, overflowing condition; not occasional outburst but characteristic speech. From Psalms 10:7 LXX.
15: ὀξεῖς οἱ πόδες αὐτῶν ἐκχέαι αἷμα (oxeis hoi podes autōn ekcheai haima) — "their feet are swift to shed blood" — oxys (sharp, swift) describes a readiness that requires no deliberation; violence is the instinctive trajectory of the unrenewed will. From Isaiah 59:7-8 LXX, where the same indictment falls on Israel specifically.
16: σύντριμμα καὶ ταλαιπωρία (syntrimma kai talaipōria) — "destruction and misery" — syntrimma (a crushing together, from trib = rubbing) is what the swift feet leave behind; the violence that the tongue and feet produce creates a trail of crushed and suffering people. The social consequences of universal unrighteousness are not abstract but embodied in human suffering.
17: ὁδὸν εἰρήνης οὐκ ἔγνωσαν (hodon eirēnēs ouk egnōsan) — "the way of peace they have not known" — the aorist egnōsan (they knew) marks this as a definitively absent knowledge; shalom as the experience of right relationships (with God, self, neighbor, creation) is unknown to the unrighteous. The road imagery reinforces the navigational language of v12 (all turned aside).
18: οὐκ ἔστιν φόβος θεοῦ ἀπέναντι τῶν ὀφθαλμῶν αὐτῶν (ouk estin phobos theou apenanti tōn ophthalmōn autōn) — "there is no fear of God before their eyes" — phobos theou (fear of God) is the OT term for the foundational orientation of wisdom and righteousness (Psalms 111:10; Proverbs 1:7; Romans 9:10). Its absence before their eyes is the summary diagnosis: the entire inner life is organized without reference to the holy God. Quoting Psalms 36:1 LXX.
19: ἵνα πᾶν στόμα φραγῇ (hina pan stoma phragē) — "that every mouth may be stopped" — aorist passive subjunctive of phrassō (to fence off, block); the closing of every mouth before God is the forensic consequence of the catena: the OT's own verdict leaves no room for self-defense. ὑπόδικος γένηται πᾶς ὁ κόσμος τῷ θεῷ — "that all the world may become accountable to God" — hypodikos = "under sentence, answerable to" (hypo = under; dikē = justice/judgment).
20:
ἐξ ἔργων νόμου οὐ δικαιωθήσεται πᾶσα σάρξ (ex ergōn nomou ou dikaiōthēsetai pasa sarx) — "from works of law no flesh will be justified" — the future passive declarative: the eschatological judgment will produce no acquittals on the basis of law-performance. πᾶσα σάρξ (pasa sarx) — "all flesh" — LXX idiom for every human being, emphasizing the frailty and mortality of the human creature before the holy God.

διὰ γὰρ νόμου ἐπίγνωσις ἁμαρτίας (dia gar nomou epignōsis hamartias) — "for through the law comes knowledge of sin" — not gnōsis (knowledge) but epignōsis (full/accurate knowledge); the law does not merely inform about sin but produces a precise recognition of its character and extent. This is the law's true diagnostic function — not justification but conviction.
21:
νυνὶ δέ (nyni de) — "but now" — the most emphatic temporal hinge in the letter; the de (but/and) marks the adversative contrast with the law's inability (v20), and nyni (now) marks the new epoch. The disclosure of God's righteousness is both temporal (happening now in the present era) and logical (here is the alternative to works-of-law).

χωρὶς νόμου (chōris nomou) — "apart from law" — not "against law" or "replacing law" but "independent of law as a mechanism of acquittal." The preposition chōris (apart from, without) is precise: the law is not the instrument through which this righteousness operates. μαρτυρουμένη (marturoumenē) — "being witnessed to" — present passive participle; the law and prophets are not being bypassed but are themselves bearing witness to what is now disclosed.
22: πίστεως Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (pisteōs Iēsou Christou) — the central syntactical debate: is this "faith in Jesus Christ" (objective genitive, the traditional reading) or "faithfulness of Jesus Christ" (subjective genitive, understanding Christ himself as the faithful one)? Grammatically both are possible; contextually Paul's consistent emphasis on the human act of believing (v22b: "to all who believe") favors the objective reading, but the subjective reading captures something theologically true (Christ's own faithfulness in obedience to death) that Paul develops in Romans 5:12-21.
23: πάντες γὰρ ἥμαρτον (pantes gar hēmarton) — "for all sinned" — aorist indicative: a single historical act encompassing all humanity. The aorist may point to the Adam-event (all sinned in Adam, Romans 5:12) rather than merely to individual moral failures. ὑστεροῦνται τῆς δόξης τοῦ θεοῦ (hysterounta tēs doxēs tou theou) — "fall short of the glory of God" — present middle/passive, continuous state. The glory of God lost in Adam's fall is the OT background (kebod yhwh); Paul sees the present condition of humanity as the ongoing consequence of that original loss.
24:
δωρεὰν (dōrean) — "freely, as a gift, gratis." The adverb emphasizes that the justification costs the recipient nothing — it is entirely given without merit or payment on the receiver's part. Notably the same word appears in John 15:25 ("they hated me without reason") and John 1:16 ("grace upon grace"); dōrean means "at no cost" to the one receiving.

ἀπολυτρώσεως (apolytrōseōs) — "redemption" — the lytron root means ransom price paid to secure release (of a slave or prisoner of war). The apo- prefix emphasizes the resultant freedom: the redemption that liberates. The price is specified in v25 (Christ's blood); the mechanism (the propitiation) and the means (faith) together constitute the ground of the free justification.
25:
ἱλαστήριον (hilastērion) — the most debated single term in Romans. Three primary options: (1) the mercy seat (kapporeth) of Exodus 25:17-22 and Lev 16, where the LXX uses this exact word — making Christ the place where God and humanity meet in the blood-applied atonement; (2) a propitiatory sacrifice (the sin-covering/guilt-removing offering); (3) a means of expiation (removing the defilement of sin without the propitiation element). The mercy seat background has the advantage of being the LXX term and providing a spatial-priestly image: Christ is both the priest and the meeting-place of divine presence and human need.

ἐν τῇ ἀνοχῇ τοῦ θεοῦ (en tē anochē tou theou) — "in the forbearance of God" — the same word as Romans 2:4; divine patience during the period of prior sins was not moral indifference but the postponement of the full accounting until the cross. The OT sacrificial system operated on "credit" secured by the coming atonement.
26: εἰς τὸ εἶναι αὐτὸν δίκαιον καὶ δικαιοῦντα τὸν ἐκ πίστεως Ἰησοῦ (eis to einai auton dikaion kai dikaiounta ton ek pisteōs Iēsou) — "so that he might be just and the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus." The pair dikaion kai dikaiounta resolves the divine dilemma: the cross satisfies the demand of God's holiness (he remains just) while simultaneously enabling the acquittal of the ungodly (he is the justifier). Without the cross, God could be just or merciful but not both simultaneously; the atonement is the resolution of this tension in Paul's theology.
27: διὰ ποίου νόμου; (dia poiou nomou;) — "through what kind of law?" — the unexpected use of nomos for the "law of faith" is a wordplay: the law of works and the law of faith are both nomoi (principles) but of opposite character. Faith as a nomos excludes boasting by its very nature — it credits what is given, not what is earned. ἐξεκλείσθη (exekleisthē) — aorist passive: boasting has been definitively shut out, excluded (from the same root as the shutting of every mouth in v19).
28: λογιζόμεθα γὰρ δικαιοῦσθαι πίστει ἄνθρωπον (logizometha gar dikaiousthai pistei anthrōpon) — "for we reckon that a person is justified by faith." Logizometha (present middle: we are reckoning, we count it as established) is the same forensic-accounting verb that governs the whole argument of Romans 4. χωρὶς ἔργων νόμου — "apart from works of law" — Paul will add "alone" by implication; Luther's famous sola fide translation ("by faith alone") is a legitimate interpretive rendering of the choris ergōn nomou exclusion.
29: ἢ Ἰουδαίων ὁ θεὸς μόνον; (ē Ioudaiōn ho theos monon;) — "or is God the God of Jews only?" — the rhetorical question surfaces the implication of the faith-principle: a God who justifies by faith (rather than by ethnic-covenant membership) is by definition a God of all peoples who believe. The monotheism of v30 ("since God is one") is the theological ground of the universality of faith-justification.
30: εἴπερ εἷς ὁ θεός (eiper heis ho theos) — "since indeed God is one" — the Shema (Deuteronomy 6:4 — yhwh echad) is the ground of Paul's argument for universal salvation by faith. One God means one way of right-standing before that God — faith — applicable to Jew and Gentile alike. The monotheism of Israel's confession becomes the basis for the universality of the gospel.
31: νόμον οὖν καταργοῦμεν διὰ τῆς πίστεως; (nomon oun katargoumen dia tēs pisteōs;) — "do we therefore nullify the law through faith?" — katargeō (render inoperative) — the anticipated objection after Romans 3's argument. Paul's denial is emphatic: μὴ γένοιτο· ἀλλὰ νόμον ἱστάνομεν — "by no means! On the contrary, we establish the law." Histanomen (we establish/uphold) — faith does not abolish the law but fulfills the purpose the law was always pointing toward, and confirms the law's own testimony (Romans 3:21 — the law and prophets witnessed to this righteousness).
